title: Metalization of topological insulators
authors: Xian-Peng Zhang, Yan-Qing Feng, Ji-Feng Shao, Haiwen Liu, Yugui Yao
abstract: In modern condensed matter theory, phases of electronic matter--such as metals and insulators-are fundamentally distinguished by the presence or absence of charge-carrying quasiparticles or excitations near the Fermi surface at low temperatures. Here, we show that this criterion breaks down in Berry-curvature-dominated systems, where transport is governed by interband coherence across the entire Fermi sea. We develop a microscopic theory of quantum transport in bulk topological insulators with a vanishing density of states at the Fermi energy, for which the conventional Drude contribution is absent. We demonstrate that impurity-scattering-induced coherence decay generates a distinct longitudinal transport channel even in the topologically trivial regime, with edge contributions rigorously excluded. This mechanism yields a finite longitudinal conductivity even in the absence of carriers at the Fermi level and exhibits an unconventional scaling linear in impurity density in the dilute limit, in stark contrast to Drude behaviour. Importantly, this decoherence-induced conductance is inversely proportional to temperature, reminiscent of strange-metal behaviour, most prominently observed in cuprate superconductors above their critical temperature. Our findings reveal quantum decoherence as a fundamental origin of longitudinal transport beyond the Drude paradigm, challenging the traditional distinction between metals and insulators.
summary: This paper challenges the traditional distinction between metals and insulators by showing that longitudinal transport can exist even when there are no charge carriers at the Fermi surface. By focusing on Berry-curvature-dominated systems like topological insulators, the authors show that impurity scattering destroys interband coherence, creating a new conduction channel. This mechanism leads to unconventional scaling, such as conductivity being linear in impurity density and inversely proportional to temperature. These findings suggest that quantum decoherence can be a primary driver of electrical response, potentially linking topological insulators to the physics of strange metals.
main_problem: Investigating whether longitudinal transport can occur in Berry-curvature-dominated systems, such as topological insulators, even when the density of states at the Fermi energy vanishes.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that impurity-induced quantum decoherence creates a longitudinal transport channel that persists even in the absence of carriers at the Fermi level, exhibiting a scaling linear in impurity density and a 1/T temperature dependence.
method: A microscopic theory using a quantum master equation framework to analyze the decay of interband coherence (off-diagonal density matrix elements) due to impurity scattering.
model_or_system: The study focuses on bulk topological insulators, specifically utilizing the Bernevig-Hughes-Zhang (BHZ) model for 2D systems, including a Hamiltonian with electronic structure, external electric field coupling, and short-range impurity potential.
key_observables: Longitudinal conductivity, longitudinal resistance, and interband coherence.
important_parameters: Impurity density (ni), impurity strength (U), Fermi energy (epsilon_F), temperature (T), and the mass parameter (M).
main_assumptions: The theory assumes short-range impurity potential and models decoherence via ordinary and anomalous-type scattering rates.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows conductivity dependence on Fermi energy and temperature; Figure 3 illustrates scaling with impurity density and mass parameter; Figure 4 compares temperature and impurity density dependence of total conductivity.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the breakdown of the Drude paradigm, develops a microscopic theory of decoherence-induced transport, demonstrates the mechanism in both topological and trivial regimes, and compares the results to known scaling laws like strange-metal behavior.
why_interesting: The paper explores how quantum decoherence acts as a fundamental mechanism for transport, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and the emergence of classical-like transport from quantum dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Coherent transport pathways: when an electron occupies coherent superpositions of conduction- and valence- band states, it can effectively bridge the large bulk gap of the topological insulator. (b) The double-slit interference experi- ment: under perfectly coherent conditions, destructive inter- ference initially produces a dark fringe, whereas introducing disturbances can lead to the appearance of bright fringe.

Figure 2. (a,b) Fermi energy (ϵF ) dependence of the longitu- dinal charge conductivities derived from (a) ordinary and (b) anomalous off-diagonal density matrix for different values of impurity density ni. Panel (c) and (d) plot the corresponding temperature T dependence. Other parameters are from the experimental results Ref. [28]: M = 5meV, Aπ/a = 1.1eV and B(π/a)2 = 7.0eV, U/a2 = 23eV and a = 0.65nm.
Figure 4. (a) T dependence of σdia L , σoff L = σ∥ L +σ⊥ L , and σL = σdia L + σoff L . Panel (b) plots the corresponding ni dependence. Other parameters are the same as Fig. 2.

Figure 3. (a-b) The longitudinal charge conductivities derived from (a) ordinary and (b) anomalous off-diagonal density ma- trix as a function of impurity density ni for several values of Fermi energy (ϵF ). (c-d) The corresponding longitudinal charge conductivities for several values of M at ϵF = 0. Other parameters are the same as Fig. 2.
